heading: Assessment
html:
Background and interpretation framework

In this pathway, liver blood tests are interpreted in 3 groups: enzymes (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT), bilirubin, and synthetic function (albumin, INR/PT). Enzyme abnormalities usually indicate liver or biliary injury pattern; synthetic markers indicate how well the liver is functioning. This distinction is clinically important because impaired synthetic function generally signals higher-risk disease even when enzyme elevation is modest.

  • Hepatocellular pattern: ALT/AST predominant rise.
  • Cholestatic pattern: ALP with raised GGT (consider biliary/infiltrative causes).
  • Isolated ALP: consider non-hepatic causes (especially bone/vitamin D context) before assuming liver disease.
  • Isolated bilirubin: separate unconjugated and conjugated context; unconjugated isolated rise without anaemia may fit Gilbert pattern.
  • Mixed pattern: common in practice; use predominant trend plus clinical context to direct first investigations.

Features that increase concern

  • Jaundice with low albumin or raised INR/PT, ascites, encephalopathy, bleeding, or severe systemic illness.
  • Progressive cholestatic picture, marked or rapidly worsening derangement, or persistent unexplained abnormalities.
  • Worsening trend over time despite addressing reversible factors.

Focused primary care assessment

  • History: alcohol, medicines/supplements, viral exposure risks, metabolic syndrome features, autoimmune and family history clues.
  • Examination: BMI, hepatosplenomegaly, stigmata of chronic liver disease, oedema/ascites, encephalopathy signs.
  • Baseline tests: FBC, U&E, albumin, bilirubin, ALT, AST, ALP, GGT; add INR/PT where indicated.
  • Pattern-directed investigations: liver aetiology screen, ultrasound where indicated, and non-invasive fibrosis risk triage in likely steatotic/alcohol-related disease.

heading: Primary care management
html:
Management should be pattern-led and time-bounded, with clear escalation if risk increases.

  • ALT/AST-predominant abnormalities: review alcohol and medicines early, assess metabolic and viral risk, and complete cause-finding tests. Escalate if marked, rising, or persistent despite intervention.
  • ALP/GGT-predominant abnormalities: evaluate cholestatic causes, check imaging where indicated, and avoid over-calling hepatic disease when ALP is isolated without GGT rise.
  • Isolated GGT: prioritise alcohol/metabolic and medicine review; monitor trend and escalate if additional abnormalities or symptoms develop.
  • Isolated bilirubin: distinguish likely benign unconjugated patterns from haemolysis or hepatobiliary disease requiring further work-up.
  • Synthetic dysfunction markers: low albumin and/or raised INR/PT indicate potential functional impairment and should trigger urgent specialist discussion/referral.
  • Follow-up strategy: repeat low-risk abnormalities after reversible-cause intervention; continue structured monitoring for persistent stable abnormalities; escalate for worsening trend, new symptoms, or unresolved uncertainty.
  • Ongoing optimisation: document alcohol intervention, weight and cardiometabolic management, and medicine-risk review at each follow-up.

heading: Who to refer
html:
Urgent same-day referral

  • Jaundice with synthetic dysfunction (raised INR/PT or low albumin), ascites, encephalopathy, active bleeding, or severe systemic deterioration.
  • Marked cholestatic picture with concern for obstruction or malignancy.
  • Rapidly worsening LFT profile or severe derangement with clinical concern.

Routine referral

  • Persistent unexplained abnormal LFTs despite structured primary-care work-up.
  • Evidence suggesting significant fibrosis/chronic liver disease requiring specialist staging.
  • Diagnostic uncertainty after initial pattern-led investigation.

Include in referral

  • LFT trend and synthetic markers over time.
  • Pattern classification, liver screen and ultrasound/fibrosis results.
  • Alcohol/medication history and interventions already undertaken.

heading: Who not to refer
html:
Do not routinely refer stable asymptomatic patients with mild low-risk abnormalities where a likely reversible cause is identified, no red flags are present, and a documented review plan is in place.

Continue primary-care management with lifestyle and medication optimisation, repeat testing, and timely escalation if abnormalities persist or worsen.
